title: Building a Dense Agentic AI CPU Rack Today
gist: Patrick Kennedy argues that agentic AI is fundamentally a CPU story, not a GPU story: agent frameworks run on CPUs and create net-new machine-to-machine workload, with AI bot traffic now exceeding human traffic on the internet, driving structural CPU demand across web, database, and tool-execution infrastructure. The (sponsored) piece favors AMD EPYC for the agentic era.

themes.theme: Agentic AI is a CPU story

insights.claim: Agent frameworks such as OpenClaw and Hermes run on CPUs rather than GPUs and must stay alive and responsive to react to events, making CPUs the substrate for agentic workloads.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI trade away from pure GPU exposure: persistent agent orchestration is net-new CPU demand, supporting server-CPU vendors (AMD EPYC, Intel) as agentic platforms scale.
insights.quote: These agent frameworks run on CPUs, and they need to stay alive and responsive so they can react whenever something happens.
insights.locator: Why Agentic AI is a CPU Story

insights.claim: Server CPUs have shifted from being undervalued infrastructure to ultra-important pieces, and the author believes companies that build out CPU capacity for agents now will have a real advantage.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: An early-mover framing: CPU capacity for agentic workloads is a competitive advantage, a positioning cue for infrastructure operators and CPU suppliers.
insights.quote: Server CPUs are heating up for a reason, and the companies that get ahead now will have a real advantage.

insights.claim: In any data-center cluster, CPUs are the common denominator: they sit alongside GPUs to process data and attach memory pools, and run storage nodes, control planes, Kubernetes, network switches, and even some network adapters.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Mental model that every cluster build has pervasive CPU content, broadening the CPU TAM narrative beyond direct compute into control and networking planes.
insights.quote: Whenever you build a cluster, CPUs are the common denominator.
insights.locator: Why Agentic AI is a CPU Story

insights.claim: On June 3, 2026, Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince stated that AI bot traffic has eclipsed human traffic on the internet, a trend the author says will worsen as agentic platforms join everyday workflows.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: A concrete inflection datapoint: machine traffic now exceeds human traffic, the demand basis for the entire CPU-buildout thesis and a metric to track via Cloudflare Radar.
insights.quote: Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince said that AI bot traffic has eclipsed human traffic on the Internet

insights.claim: Bot traffic hitting Cloudflare and other endpoints forces web servers, databases, and applications to handle the load, and running those bots consumes CPU horsepower across the stack.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Second-order demand: agent traffic cascades CPU load onto every endpoint it touches, widening the CPU demand beyond where agents are hosted.
insights.quote: running those bots consumes CPU horsepower
insights.locator: CPUs For Agentic AI From Bare Metal To Sandboxes

insights.claim: For deterministic, reproducible results the author recommends having the LLM generate scripts that run deterministically on the CPU rather than relying on raw LLM output to execute commands directly.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: An architectural pattern shifting work from probabilistic GPU inference to deterministic CPU execution, reinforcing CPU demand and reducing token spend.
insights.quote: ask the LLM to generate scripts that run tasks deterministically on the CPU rather than relying on raw LLM output to execute commands directly
insights.locator: 'LLM inference usually runs over APIs to GPUs.'

insights.claim: In experiments feeding a sudo SSH workflow to LLMs, around 40% of initial calls used to fail on malformed quotations; with newer models like GPT-5.5, Qwen3.5-397B, Fable-5 and Opus-4.8, about 25% of workflows still loop to fix broken SSH commands from simple formatting errors, burning tokens.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Quantifies hidden token/time cost (~25% loop rate even on frontier models) of letting LLMs format commands directly, making the case for deterministic tooling and CPU offload.
insights.quote: With newer models, we still see 25 percent of workflows looping to fix broken SSH commands from simple formatting errors.

insights.claim: Building a purpose-built tool for a specific access path lets the LLM format only the variable call while the common SSH command parts run deterministically on the CPU, keeping the agent on track.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Concrete engineering pattern (thin LLM formatting + deterministic CPU tool) that reduces error loops, again pushing work onto CPUs.
insights.quote: The LLM formats the call, while the common parts of the SSH command get handled deterministically by the tool.
insights.locator: 'Building a tool for that specific access path fixes much of the problem.'

insights.claim: The author advises deploying company agents like onboarding a contractor with constrained access to data and services, not open all-access privileges, labeling the all-access approach a 'YOLO AI agent.'
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: A least-privilege mental model for enterprise agent deployment, relevant to security tooling and governance demand as agents proliferate.
insights.quote: When you deploy at a company, think about it more like bringing on a contractor.

insights.claim: Agentic deployment is shifting toward short-lived sandboxes that are built, issue commands, and are torn down, and the key factors determining agent performance have nothing to do with the LLM itself.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Performance bottleneck is infrastructure (sandbox spin-up/teardown on CPUs), not model quality, redirecting investment focus toward the compute/orchestration layer.
insights.quote: The key factors that determine how well your agents perform have nothing to do with the LLM itself.
insights.locator: CPUs For Agentic AI From Bare Metal To Sandboxes

insights.claim: Today's infrastructure is optimized for a population of just over 8B people, but new infrastructure must serve people plus likely significantly more agents, requiring applications to be optimized for machine-to-machine rather than human-to-machine traffic.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Frames a multi-year re-architecting of application infrastructure for machine-scale request volumes far exceeding the human population, a large structural demand thesis.
insights.quote: applications need to be optimized for machine-to-machine traffic, not just human-to-machine traffic
insights.locator: CPUs For Agentic AI Is Like From Mail Order To Electronic Orders

insights.claim: Agents have timeout windows and will not wait days, so infrastructure must evolve to deliver near-instant responses, analogous to how automating quote/deal pricing let a vendor win deals over a slower competitor purely on speed.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Latency-to-agents becomes a competitive moat: firms whose infrastructure responds fast to agent requests win, echoing past speed-driven sales advantages.
insights.quote: Agents will have timeout windows, just like channel partners and customers did. They are not going to wait days for quotes.
insights.locator: 'I always think of that when I think of agentic AI.'

insights.claim: When agents hit front-end applications backed by per-license databases like Oracle or SQL Server, the author predicts firms will eventually emerge to optimize licensing for the agentic-AI era with legacy applications, a topic the industry has not yet started discussing.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: An unaddressed cost vector: surging agent request volume could explode per-seat/per-core database license costs, creating a new niche for licensing-optimization services and a risk for legacy-DB-dependent firms.
insights.quote: there will be firms that go out and optimize licensing for the era of agentic AI with legacy applications
insights.locator: STH The Other Side Of Agentic CPU Workflows

insights.claim: The piece is explicitly sponsored (AMD supplied the CPUs, Dell paid the author's travel) and uses AMD EPYC and Dell servers, with the author signaling STH will be talking a lot about AMD EPYC over the next year.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Disclosed AMD/Dell sponsorship plus a stated forward emphasis on AMD EPYC signals a bullish editorial tilt toward AMD server CPUs as the agentic-CPU beneficiary; weigh for promotional bias.
insights.quote: if you read the STH Substack, it is pretty clear why we will be talking a lot about AMD EPYC in the next year and change
insights.locator: 'We have a video for this one.'
